pattern: A nested-list grammar with only integers, commas, and brackets can be decoded with a single left-to-right scan by treating '[' and ']' as push/pop signals and everything else as accumulating digits of a number.
hints: Read the string once from left to right; you never need to look backward.
Whenever you see '[', you are starting a new list; whenever you see ']', the list you were building is finished and belongs to whatever list (or nothing) came before it.

Use recursion (or an explicit stack) keyed off '[' and ']': a bare run of digits (with an optional leading '-') is an integer, anything inside a bracket pair is a list of comma-separated recursively-parsed items, and an immediate '[]' is an empty list.

edge_inputs.description: bare non-negative integer with no brackets at all
bare negative integer with no brackets
nested list mixing depths and negative values
an empty list nested as a non-first element between integers
list of only empty lists, no integers anywhere
sibling lists at the same level with differing lengths and a negative number spanning multiple digits

stress.description: randomly generated nested-list strings of varying shape and depth
stress.note: size scales how many top-level siblings are packed into one list string; depth is capped locally so recursion stays shallow
exercises the top-level-integer branch (no brackets) across the full value range
